content.problemStatement:
In this worked out example, we have created a simple Library Card System using Django's One-to-One relationship. 
We have two models: `Patron` and `LibraryCard`. Each Patron can have only one LibraryCard, and each LibraryCard belongs to only one Patron.

#### In the `models.py` 
Where the Patron and LibraryCard models are defined, we establish a one-to-one relationship between them as follows:

```python
......
class LibraryCard(models.Model):
    patron = models.OneToOneField(Patron, on_delete=models.CASCADE)
......   
```
Here, \
`OneToOneField(Patron, ...)`: This means we are connecting the LibraryCard to the Patron model using a one-to-one link.
`on_delete=models.CASCADE`: This means that if the Patron is deleted, their LibraryCard will also be automatically deleted.

#### In the `views.py` file:
We have created sample Patrons and Library Cards. \
Notice how the Library cards are linked to patrons using the relationship established before. \
Eg.
```python 
LibraryCard.objects.create(patron=p1, card_number="LC1001")
```
After this, all the patrons are fetched and are passed to the template `homepage.html` which you can see when you click on **RUN** (which runs the migrations and starts the server)
